enrichment.short_description: Payoneer is a publicly traded (NASDAQ: PAYO) global payments platform providing multi-currency accounts, cross-border payouts, and receivables infrastructure for freelancers, SMBs, and marketplaces in 190+ countries.
enrichment.starting_price: Free account (currency conversion and withdrawal fees apply)
enrichment.seo_title: Payoneer Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Payoneer review covering features, fees, pricing plans, pros and cons, and alternatives for cross-border payments and global payouts in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Payoneer is a publicly traded financial technology company (Nasdaq: PAYO) that provides cross-border payment infrastructure for businesses, freelancers, and marketplaces. Founded in 2005 by Yuval Tal and headquartered in New York City, Payoneer built its early business around prepaid cards for freelancers and expanded into a multi-currency account platform that lets users receive payments as though they held local bank accounts in the United States, United Kingdom, European Union, Japan, and other major markets, then withdraw, convert, or spend those funds. The company went public via a SPAC merger in 2021. Payoneer's core account is free to open; the company earns revenue primarily through currency conversion spreads, withdrawal fees, and small percentage fees on payments received from marketplaces and B2B partners such as Amazon, Airbnb, Fiverr, Upwork, and Walmart. Payoneer also offers Payoneer Checkout for online sellers, working-capital advances for e-commerce merchants, and a prepaid corporate card. Its target users are freelancers, e-commerce sellers, agencies, and small-to-midsize businesses that need to send or receive money internationally without maintaining foreign bank accounts. Payoneer differentiates itself from consumer-focused wallets by emphasizing B2B receivables, marketplace payouts, and local receiving accounts in 190-plus countries and territories, along with mass-payout tools for enterprises paying large numbers of overseas contractors or sellers. Key competitors include PayPal, a broader consumer-and-business payments platform; Wise, known for low-cost currency transfers; Skrill; and Airwallex. Payoneer reports serving millions of customers worldwide and continues to grow through partnerships with global marketplaces that rely on it as a default payout method for non-US sellers. Because pricing is largely fee-based rather than subscription-based, prospective users should review Payoneer's fee schedule for their specific currency corridor and withdrawal method before committing, as costs vary by account type, country, and transaction volume. Payoneer's investor base and public listing mean its quarterly results, transaction volume, and customer growth metrics are disclosed publicly, which is uncommon among cross-border payment providers and gives businesses a way to evaluate the company's long-term stability before relying on it for critical payment infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Payoneer is a global payments company that lets freelancers, e-commerce sellers, and businesses receive, hold, and move money across borders without needing a foreign bank account. Founded in 2005 and headquartered in New York City, Payoneer has grown from a prepaid-card business into a publicly traded (Nasdaq: PAYO) payments infrastructure provider used by millions of customers worldwide.

The platform is best known for local receiving accounts that let sellers on marketplaces like Amazon, Upwork, and Fiverr collect payments as if they held a bank account in the buyer's country, then convert and withdraw the funds through Payoneer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Payoneer's core product is its multi-currency account, which supports local receiving accounts in major markets and a prepaid Mastercard for spending or withdrawing funds. Businesses can also use Payoneer Checkout to accept payments from global customers on their own websites.

For larger organizations, Payoneer offers mass payout tools and an API that let platforms and marketplaces pay thousands of overseas contractors, sellers, or affiliates from a single dashboard, along with optional working-capital advances for eligible e-commerce sellers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Payoneer does not charge a monthly subscription fee for its core account; both individual and business accounts are free to open. Instead, Payoneer generates revenue through currency conversion spreads, withdrawal fees, and small percentage fees on certain marketplace or B2B payments.

Because fees vary by country, currency corridor, and payment source, actual costs depend heavily on how and where a user sends or receives money. Payoneer Checkout and enterprise mass-payout services are typically priced on a custom basis for higher-volume users.

features.title: Multi-currency receiving accounts
features.description: Local receiving accounts in the US, UK, EU, Japan, and other markets let users get paid like a local without a foreign bank account.

features.title: Marketplace payouts integration
features.description: Deep integrations with Amazon, Airbnb, Upwork, Fiverr, and other marketplaces make Payoneer a default payout option for global sellers and freelancers.

features.title: Currency conversion
features.description: Convert received funds into a local currency before withdrawing to a linked bank account.

features.title: Payoneer Checkout
features.description: An e-commerce payment acceptance product letting online sellers accept cards and local payment methods from global customers.

features.title: Mass payouts for businesses
features.description: Enterprises can pay large numbers of contractors, sellers, or affiliates in different countries from a single dashboard.

features.title: Prepaid Mastercard
features.description: A physical and virtual Payoneer card lets users spend received funds directly or withdraw cash at ATMs.

features.title: Working capital
features.description: Eligible e-commerce sellers can access short-term capital advances against future receivables.

features.title: Payouts API
features.description: Marketplaces and platforms can embed Payoneer payment infrastructure directly into their own products.

pros: Free to open an account, with no monthly subscription fee for the core service
Wide network covering 190+ countries with local receiving accounts in major currencies
Deep integration with major freelance and e-commerce marketplaces
Publicly traded company with transparent financial reporting
Mass payout tools suited to enterprises paying many international recipients
cons: Currency conversion and withdrawal fees can add up for frequent users
Customer support response times are inconsistent according to user reviews
Account holds and verification requirements can delay access to funds
Less suited to casual peer-to-peer payments than consumer wallets like PayPal
Fee structures vary by country and account type, making costs hard to predict upfront

faqs.question: Is Payoneer free to use?
faqs.answer: Opening a Payoneer account is free; the company charges fees for currency conversion, withdrawals, and some marketplace-specific transactions rather than a subscription fee.

faqs.question: How is Payoneer different from PayPal?
faqs.answer: Payoneer focuses on B2B cross-border receivables, marketplace payouts, and local receiving accounts, while PayPal is a broader consumer-and-business wallet built around peer-to-peer and checkout payments.

faqs.question: Is Payoneer publicly traded?
faqs.answer: Yes, Payoneer Global Inc. trades on Nasdaq under the ticker PAYO after going public via a SPAC merger in 2021.

faqs.question: What currencies does Payoneer support?
faqs.answer: Payoneer supports receiving and holding balances in major currencies such as USD, EUR, GBP, and JPY, with local receiving accounts in several markets.

faqs.question: Which marketplaces integrate with Payoneer?
faqs.answer: Amazon, Airbnb, Upwork, Fiverr, Walmart, and many other global marketplaces offer Payoneer as a payout option for sellers and freelancers.

faqs.question: Does Payoneer offer a debit card?
faqs.answer: Yes, Payoneer issues a prepaid Mastercard that lets customers spend or withdraw received funds.

faqs.question: Who founded Payoneer?
faqs.answer: Payoneer was founded in 2005 by Yuval Tal.

faqs.question: Where is Payoneer headquartered?
faqs.answer: Payoneer is headquartered in New York City, with offices in more than 20 countries.

use_cases.title: Enterprise mass payouts

Local receiving accounts in multiple currencies
Fees apply for currency conversion and withdrawals
Mass payout tools for paying multiple recipients
Fees apply per transaction and currency conversion
Support for cards and local payment methods
Custom pricing based on transaction volume

enrichment.short_description: PayPal is a publicly traded (NASDAQ: PYPL) global online payments company offering consumer digital wallets, peer-to-peer transfers, and business checkout and invoicing tools.
enrichment.starting_price: Free personal account (transaction fees apply for business use and certain transfers)
enrichment.seo_title: PayPal Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PayPal review for 2026 covering features, fees, business and personal pricing, pros, cons, and alternatives to the global online payments platform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PayPal is one of the world's largest online payment companies, publicly traded on Nasdaq under the ticker PYPL. It traces its origins to Confinity, founded in December 1998 by Peter Thiel, Max Levchin, and Luke Nosek, which merged in 2000 with Elon Musk's X.com; the combined company was rebranded PayPal in 2001 and went public that same year before being acquired by eBay in 2002 for about 1.5 billion dollars. PayPal operated as an eBay subsidiary until it was spun off as an independent public company again in July 2015. Headquartered in San Jose, California, PayPal now employs roughly 23,000 people worldwide and serves hundreds of millions of active consumer and merchant accounts. The platform lets individuals send and receive money, shop online with a linked bank account, card, or PayPal balance, and split purchases using its Pay in 4 buy-now-pay-later product, while merchants use PayPal Checkout, invoicing, and its Braintree payment processing subsidiary to accept payments on websites and apps. PayPal also owns Venmo, a popular US peer-to-peer payment app, and Xoom, an international money transfer service. Pricing is largely freemium and transaction-based: opening a personal account is free, and PayPal earns revenue mainly through per-transaction fees charged to merchants and on certain money transfers, currency conversion, and instant-transfer features. This differs from Payoneer, a payments company that focuses more narrowly on B2B cross-border receivables and marketplace seller payouts with local receiving accounts, whereas PayPal is a broader consumer-facing wallet and checkout brand with deep integration across e-commerce sites. PayPal's target users span everyday consumers making online purchases, small businesses and freelancers invoicing clients, and large enterprises processing payments through Braintree. Its main competitors include Stripe and Square (Block) for merchant payment processing, Payoneer and Wise for cross-border transfers, and Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Zelle for consumer digital payments. PayPal continues to compete by expanding buy-now-pay-later offerings, cryptocurrency support, and its own debit and credit card products, positioning itself as a full-stack consumer and commerce payments ecosystem rather than a single-purpose tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PayPal is a publicly traded online payments company that lets consumers pay online, send money to friends and family, and lets merchants accept payments on their websites and apps. It traces its roots to Confinity, founded in 1998, and has operated as an independent public company (Nasdaq: PYPL) since its 2015 spinoff from eBay.

Headquartered in San Jose, California, PayPal is one of the most widely recognized names in digital payments, with deep integration across online retailers worldwide and ownership of Venmo, a leading US peer-to-peer payment app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Consumers use PayPal to shop online, send peer-to-peer transfers, and split purchases with the Pay in 4 buy-now-pay-later option. A linked PayPal or Venmo card also lets users spend their balance directly.

Merchants rely on PayPal Checkout and invoicing tools for smaller businesses, while larger platforms use Braintree, PayPal's payment processing subsidiary, to accept cards and digital wallets at scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Opening a personal PayPal account is free, and there is no monthly subscription fee for standard use. PayPal instead earns revenue through per-transaction fees charged to merchants, along with fees on certain transfers, currency conversion, and instant withdrawals.
Business and Braintree pricing scales with transaction volume and payment methods accepted, and larger merchants can negotiate custom rates directly with PayPal.

features.title: PayPal Checkout

features.title: Peer-to-peer transfers
features.description: Send money instantly to friends and family using an email address or phone number.

features.title: Pay in 4
features.description: A buy-now-pay-later option that lets shoppers split eligible purchases into four installments.

features.title: Invoicing
features.description: Create and send professional invoices for freelancers and small businesses to collect client payments.

features.title: Braintree integration
features.description: A full-stack payment processing platform for larger merchants needing cards, wallets, and local payment methods.

features.title: Venmo integration
features.description: Social peer-to-peer payments popular among US consumers, owned and operated by PayPal.

features.title: Currency conversion
features.description: Accept and convert international payments for cross-border e-commerce.

features.title: PayPal and Venmo cards
features.description: Debit and credit cards that let users spend their PayPal or Venmo balance directly.

pros: Extremely wide merchant and consumer adoption worldwide
Free to open a personal account with no monthly fee
Buyer and seller protection programs on eligible purchases
Broad product range spanning Checkout, Braintree, Venmo, invoicing, and Pay in 4
Recognized, trusted brand that can improve online checkout conversion
cons: Transaction fees can be higher than some competitors for small businesses
Account holds or freezes are a recurring user complaint
Customer support can be slow to resolve disputes
Currency conversion rates include a markup over market rates

faqs.question: Is PayPal free?
faqs.answer: Personal accounts are free to open; PayPal earns revenue from merchant transaction fees and certain transfer or conversion fees.

faqs.question: Who founded PayPal?
faqs.answer: Confinity, PayPal's predecessor, was founded in 1998 by Peter Thiel, Max Levchin, and Luke Nosek; it merged with X.com in 2000 and adopted the PayPal name in 2001.

faqs.question: Is PayPal publicly traded?
faqs.answer: Yes, PayPal Holdings trades on Nasdaq under the ticker PYPL and has been independent since its 2015 spinoff from eBay.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PayPal and Payoneer?
faqs.answer: PayPal is primarily a consumer wallet and merchant checkout brand, while Payoneer focuses on B2B cross-border payouts and marketplace seller receivables.

faqs.question: Does PayPal own Venmo?
faqs.answer: Yes, Venmo is a PayPal-owned peer-to-peer payment app popular in the United States.

faqs.question: What is Braintree?
faqs.answer: Braintree is PayPal's payment processing subsidiary used by larger merchants and platforms to accept card and digital wallet payments.

faqs.question: Where is PayPal headquartered?
faqs.answer: PayPal is headquartered in San Jose, California.

faqs.question: Does PayPal offer buy now pay later?
faqs.answer: Yes, through its Pay in 4 installment product.

enrichment.short_description: pCloud is a Swiss cloud storage service known for offering one-time lifetime payment plans alongside traditional subscriptions, with optional client-side encryption.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (10 GB); paid plans from $4.99/month or $199 one-time lifetime
enrichment.seo_title: pCloud Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: pCloud review covering lifetime and subscription pricing, encryption, features, pros and cons, and alternatives to this Swiss cloud storage service.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: pCloud is a cloud storage and file-sync service founded in 2013 and headquartered in Baar, Switzerland, which markets itself on Swiss data-protection law and, most distinctively, on one-time lifetime payment plans rather than requiring an ongoing subscription. Users can pay once for 500 GB, 2 TB, or 10 TB of storage that remains active for as long as pCloud operates, an unusual pricing model in a cloud storage market otherwise dominated by monthly or annual subscriptions from providers like Google Drive, Dropbox, and Microsoft OneDrive. pCloud also offers standard monthly and annual subscription plans starting around 4.99 dollars per month for 500 GB, alongside a free tier that provides 10 GB of storage. Its core product lets users sync files across desktop, mobile, and web, stream media directly from cloud storage with a built-in player, and share files and folders via links. A notable add-on, pCloud Encryption (often called Crypto), provides client-side, zero-knowledge encryption for a designated folder, ensuring that not even pCloud can read the contents; this is sold separately from base storage rather than bundled by default. pCloud stores customer data in either European Union or United States data centers depending on the plan and account settings, giving privacy-conscious users a degree of choice. The service targets individuals, families, photographers, and small businesses who want predictable, long-term storage costs without recurring subscription fees, as well as users specifically drawn to Swiss privacy positioning. Key competitors include Dropbox, Google Drive, Microsoft OneDrive, and other lifetime-plan-oriented rivals such as Internxt and IceDrive, though pCloud is one of the longer-running and most widely recognized providers offering the lifetime-purchase model at scale. Because the lifetime plans require a large upfront payment, pCloud is best suited to users confident in their long-term storage needs, while the monthly and annual subscriptions suit those who prefer lower upfront costs or want to test the service before committing. Family plans are also available, letting up to five users share a single storage allocation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: pCloud is a cloud storage provider founded in 2013 and headquartered in Baar, Switzerland, best known for offering one-time lifetime payment plans as an alternative to recurring subscriptions used by most cloud storage competitors. Users pay once for a fixed amount of storage that remains active for as long as pCloud operates.

Beyond storage, pCloud provides cross-platform sync, a built-in media player for streaming stored video and audio, and optional client-side encryption for users who want an additional layer of privacy.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: pCloud syncs files across Windows, Mac, Linux, iOS, and Android, and lets users share files and folders through public links with optional passwords and expiration dates. Its built-in media player allows streaming video and audio directly from cloud storage without downloading files first.

For added privacy, pCloud Encryption (Crypto) is available as a paid add-on that encrypts a designated folder client-side so that not even pCloud can read its contents. Users can also choose whether their data is stored in EU or US data centers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: pCloud offers a free tier with 10 GB of storage, monthly and annual subscription plans starting at $4.99 per month for 500 GB, and distinctive one-time lifetime plans starting at $199 for 500 GB, $399 for 2 TB, and $1,190 for 10 TB.

The lifetime plans require a larger upfront payment but eliminate recurring fees going forward, while subscriptions offer a lower-commitment way to access the same storage tiers. A Family plan lets up to five users share one storage pool.

features.title: Lifetime one-time payment plans
features.description: Pay once for 500GB, 2TB, or 10TB of storage that lasts as long as the service operates, unlike typical subscription cloud storage.

features.title: Cross-platform sync
features.description: Desktop, mobile, and web apps keep files synced across Windows, Mac, Linux, iOS, and Android.

features.title: Built-in media player
features.description: Stream video and audio files directly from cloud storage without downloading.

features.title: pCloud Encryption (Crypto)
features.description: An optional paid add-on that gives client-side, zero-knowledge encryption for a private folder.

features.title: File sharing and links
features.description: Share files and folders via public links with optional passwords and expiration dates.

features.title: Data center choice
features.description: Store data in EU (Luxembourg) or US data centers depending on account settings.

features.title: Family plan
features.description: Share a single storage allocation with up to 5 family members.

features.title: Automatic backup
features.description: Back up folders from computers and connected cloud accounts like Google Drive or Dropbox.

pros: Unique one-time lifetime payment option avoids recurring subscription costs
Choice of EU or US data center location
Built-in media player for streaming stored video and audio
Generous storage tiers up to 10TB
Family plan lets multiple users share one allocation
cons: Zero-knowledge encryption (Crypto) is sold as a separate paid add-on rather than included by default
Large upfront cost for lifetime plans is a barrier for some users
Sync speed and interface have received mixed reviews compared to larger competitors
Fewer third-party app integrations than Google Drive or Dropbox

faqs.question: What is pCloud's lifetime plan?
faqs.answer: A one-time payment that grants a fixed amount of storage for as long as pCloud remains in operation, instead of a recurring subscription.

faqs.question: Is pCloud encrypted by default?
faqs.answer: No, base storage is not zero-knowledge encrypted; client-side encryption is available through the separate pCloud Encryption (Crypto) add-on.

faqs.question: Where is pCloud based?
faqs.answer: pCloud is headquartered in Baar, Switzerland, and was founded in 2013.

faqs.question: Can I choose where my data is stored?
faqs.answer: Yes, pCloud lets users choose between European Union and United States data centers.

faqs.question: Does pCloud have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, pCloud offers 10 GB of free storage.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pCloud and Dropbox?
faqs.answer: pCloud offers one-time lifetime payment plans and a built-in media player, while Dropbox is subscription-only and focuses more on collaboration and integrations.

faqs.question: Does pCloud support family sharing?
faqs.answer: Yes, a Family plan lets up to 5 users share one storage pool.

faqs.question: Is pCloud's lifetime plan really a one-time payment?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is a genuine one-time payment for the storage tier selected, with no recurring billing afterward.

use_cases.title: Long-term personal file backup
use_cases.description: Individuals who want predictable storage costs pay once for a lifetime plan instead of ongoing subscription fees.

use_cases.title: Media streaming and photo storage
use_cases.description: Photographers and media creators use pCloud's built-in player to store and stream large video and photo libraries.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused file storage
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious users add pCloud Encryption to keep a folder end-to-end encrypted and choose EU data center storage.

enrichment.short_description: PDF Expert is a PDF editing, annotation, and e-signature app made by Readdle, primarily for Mac, iPad, and iPhone, with AI-assisted document tools.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (view/basic annotate); Premium from EUR 5.99/week or EUR 84.99/year; Lifetime EUR 149.99 (Mac only)
enrichment.seo_title: PDF Expert Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PDF Expert review covering Readdle's Mac and iOS PDF editor, AI features, pricing plans, pros, cons, and top alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PDF Expert is a PDF editing and annotation application developed by Readdle, a software company founded in 2007 in Odesa, Ukraine, by Igor Zhadanov, Alex Tyagulsky, Andrian Budantsov, and Dmitry Protserov. Readdle is also known for other productivity apps including Spark and Scanner Pro, and PDF Expert has long been one of its flagship products, particularly popular among Mac, iPad, and iPhone users for its native Apple-platform design. The app lets users view, annotate, edit text and images, merge and split files, fill and create forms, add legally binding digital signatures, redact sensitive content, and run optical character recognition (OCR) on scanned documents. Readdle has more recently added PDF Copilot, an AI-powered assistant built into PDF Expert that can summarize documents, answer questions about their content, and help with translation and conversion to formats like Word, Excel, and PowerPoint. PDF Expert follows a freemium model: the app can be downloaded and used for free with basic viewing and limited editing, while full functionality requires a Premium subscription priced at roughly 5.99 euros per week or about 84.99 euros per year, with an option to buy a one-time Lifetime license for around 149.99 euros that is restricted to the Mac version. Students and educators can receive a discount on the annual plan. PDF Expert's target users are Apple-ecosystem professionals, students, and businesses who need a fast, native PDF editor without relying on Adobe Acrobat, especially those who prefer a one-time purchase option over perpetual subscriptions. Its main competitors include Adobe Acrobat, PDF Expert's more heavyweight and long-established rival; PDFpen by Smile; Foxit PDF Editor; and free alternatives like PDFescape and macOS Preview. PDF Expert differentiates itself through deep macOS and iOS integration, a clean interface tailored to touch and Apple Pencil input on iPad, and increasingly AI-assisted document workflows through PDF Copilot, positioning it as a lighter, more affordable option than Acrobat for users centered on Apple devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PDF Expert is a PDF editing and annotation app made by Readdle, a Ukrainian software company founded in 2007 in Odesa. The app is built primarily for Mac, iPad, and iPhone, offering a native Apple experience with support for Apple Pencil and touch-based editing.

PDF Expert combines core PDF tools such as annotation, editing, and form filling with newer AI-assisted features through PDF Copilot, positioning it as a faster and more affordable alternative to Adobe Acrobat for Apple-centric users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users can annotate, edit text and images, merge and split files, fill and design forms, add digital signatures, redact sensitive content, and run OCR on scanned documents directly within PDF Expert.

PDF Copilot, Readdle's AI assistant built into the app, can summarize documents, answer questions about their content, and assist with translation and conversion to formats like Word, Excel, and PowerPoint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: PDF Expert can be downloaded for free with basic viewing and limited editing. Full functionality requires a Premium subscription priced at roughly EUR 5.99 per week or about EUR 84.99 per year, with a student discount available on the annual plan.
A one-time Lifetime license priced around EUR 149.99 is also available, though it is restricted to the Mac version only; new feature releases after purchase may cost extra.

pros: Native, well-designed apps for Mac, iPad, and iPhone
One-time Lifetime purchase option (Mac) as an alternative to subscription
AI-powered PDF Copilot assistant built in
Fast performance and Apple Pencil support on iPad
Strong OCR and form-filling tools
cons: Lifetime license is limited to the Mac version only
Weekly and annual subscription pricing can be costly compared to some competitors
No native Windows or Android app, with a Mac/iOS/iPadOS focus
Some advanced features are gated behind the Premium subscription

faqs.question: Is PDF Expert free?
faqs.answer: A free version is available with limited editing; full features require a Premium subscription or Lifetime license.

faqs.question: Who makes PDF Expert?
faqs.answer: PDF Expert is developed by Readdle, a software company founded in 2007 in Odesa, Ukraine.

faqs.question: Does PDF Expert work on Windows?
faqs.answer: PDF Expert is primarily built for Mac, iPad, and iPhone; it does not have a native Windows app.

faqs.question: What is PDF Copilot?
faqs.answer: PDF Copilot is Readdle's AI assistant built into PDF Expert that can summarize and answer questions about document content.

faqs.question: Can I buy PDF Expert once instead of subscribing?
faqs.answer: Yes, a one-time Lifetime license is available, but only for the Mac version.

faqs.question: Does PDF Expert support e-signatures?
faqs.answer: Yes, users can add and request legally binding digital signatures.

faqs.question: Is there a student discount?
faqs.answer: Yes, Readdle offers a discount on the annual plan for verified students and educators.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PDF Expert and Adobe Acrobat?
faqs.answer: PDF Expert is a lighter, more affordable, Apple-focused alternative, while Acrobat is a more feature-heavy, cross-platform, long-established industry standard.

enrichment.short_description: PdfDing is a free, open-source, self-hosted PDF manager and viewer that lets users organize, annotate, and access PDF documents across devices via a Docker-deployed web app.
enrichment.seo_title: PdfDing Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PdfDing review covering this open-source, self-hosted PDF manager and viewer: features, Docker setup, pricing, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PdfDing is a free and open-source, self-hosted PDF manager, viewer, and lightweight editor designed to give individuals and organizations full control over their document storage instead of relying on a third-party cloud PDF service. Built with the Django web framework and distributed under an open-source license, PdfDing is developed primarily by a maintainer known as mrmn2 and hosted on both GitHub and Codeberg, where the project has attracted over 1,700 GitHub stars, reflecting notable traction within the self-hosting and privacy-focused open-source community. The project received funding support from the NGI0 Commons Fund, established by NLnet with backing from the European Commission, which supports privacy-respecting and open internet technologies. PdfDing is deployed using Docker, Docker Compose, or Helm for Kubernetes, and is designed to be minimal, fast, and easy to set up on a home server, NAS, or self-managed cloud instance. Core features include a browser-based PDF viewer that remembers your reading position across devices, multi-level organization through tagging, starring, and archiving, basic editing capabilities such as adding text, highlights, and drawings, digital signature support, and sharing via links or QR codes with optional access controls. It also supports single sign-on through OIDC and two-factor authentication for security-conscious deployments, along with dark mode and customizable themes. Because there is no company behind PdfDing and no paid tier, its pricing model is purely open source: the software itself is free, though users bear their own hosting costs such as a VPS, home server, or NAS. PdfDing's target users are privacy-conscious individuals, developers, and small teams who want a self-hosted alternative to cloud PDF readers and managers. Its closest competitors in the self-hosted document space include Paperless-ngx, which focuses more on document scanning and archiving, Stirling PDF, a self-hosted PDF editing toolkit, and general-purpose file managers like Nextcloud when used for PDF storage. PdfDing distinguishes itself by focusing specifically on reading and light editing convenience across devices rather than heavyweight document management or OCR-based archiving.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PdfDing is an open-source, self-hosted PDF manager and viewer built with Django, designed to give users full ownership of their document storage. It is deployed with Docker and has gained notable traction in the self-hosting community, with over 1,700 stars on its GitHub repository.

The project received funding support from the NGI0 Commons Fund, backed by NLnet and the European Commission, reflecting its focus on privacy-respecting, open internet technology rather than a commercial product with a company behind it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: PdfDing offers a browser-based PDF viewer that remembers reading position across devices, along with tagging, starring, and archiving tools for organizing large PDF collections. Lightweight editing lets users add text, highlights, and drawings, and digital signatures can be applied and accessed from any device.

Security-conscious deployments are supported through OIDC single sign-on and two-factor authentication, while sharing via links or QR codes with optional access control makes it easy to distribute documents to an external audience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: PdfDing is entirely free and open source, with no subscription tiers or paid version of the software itself. There is no official managed cloud offering.

The only cost users incur is their own hosting infrastructure, such as a VPS, home server, or NAS, which they choose and manage independently when self-hosting PdfDing via Docker, Docker Compose, or Helm.

Full data ownership through self-hosting

Supports SSO/OIDC and two-factor authentication for security
Actively maintained with growing community traction (1,700+ GitHub stars)
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge and infrastructure with no managed cloud version
Smaller feature set than commercial PDF editors like Adobe Acrobat or PDF Expert
No official mobile app; relies on browser access
Smaller community and support base than larger open-source document tools

faqs.question: Is PdfDing free?
faqs.answer: Yes, PdfDing is fully open source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: How do I install PdfDing?
faqs.answer: PdfDing is typically deployed using Docker, Docker Compose, or Helm for Kubernetes.

faqs.question: Does PdfDing have a hosted or cloud version?
faqs.answer: No, PdfDing is designed to be self-hosted; there is no official managed cloud offering.

faqs.question: What framework is PdfDing built on?
faqs.answer: PdfDing is built using the Django web framework.

faqs.question: Can I edit PDFs in PdfDing?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports lightweight editing such as adding text, highlights, and drawings.

faqs.question: Does PdfDing support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports OIDC-based SSO and two-factor authentication.

faqs.question: Where is the PdfDing source code hosted?
faqs.answer: On GitHub (mrmn2/PdfDing) and mirrored on Codeberg.

faqs.question: Who funds PdfDing's development?
faqs.answer: The project has received funding through the NGI0 Commons Fund, backed by NLnet and the European Commission.

enrichment.short_description: PDFescape is a free, browser-based PDF editor and form filler, now owned by Claranova's Avanquest division, offering both a free online tool and paid Premium/Ultimate tiers.
enrichment.company_size: Part of Claranova (Avanquest); standalone team size not publicly disclosed
enrichment.headquarters: Montreal, Canada (part of Claranova / Avanquest Software)
enrichment.seo_title: PDFescape Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PDFescape review covering the free online PDF editor and form filler, Premium and Ultimate pricing, features, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PDFescape is a browser-based PDF editing and form-filling tool that has offered a genuinely free online tier since its early years, distinguishing it from many competitors that gate core editing behind a paywall. It was established in Montreal, Canada, by software developers Roger Politis and Bruno Vanryb under the company CTdeveloping, LLC, with the two having earlier co-founded BVRP Software and contributed to Avanquest Software. In March 2021, PDFescape was acquired by Avanquest, the software publishing division of the French group Claranova, bringing it under a larger, publicly traded parent company. PDFescape's free online tool lets users edit PDF text and images, fill and design PDF forms, and annotate documents directly in a browser without installing software, though the free tier caps file size at 10 MB, page count at 100, limits storage to 10 files, and retains file history for only seven days while adding no watermark to prints or saves. Paid tiers remove ad interruptions and raise limits: Premium, around 3 dollars per month, increases the file-size cap to 40 MB, stores up to 100 files, and extends history to 30 days, while Ultimate, around 6 dollars per month, adds an unlimited-history desktop application with advanced tools such as PDF creation from over 300 file types, merging, compression, encryption, redaction, and e-signatures. PDFescape's target users are individuals, students, and small businesses who need occasional PDF editing or form-filling without committing to a full subscription like Adobe Acrobat. Its main competitors include Adobe Acrobat, Smallpdf, PDF Expert, and other browser-based tools such as Sejda and DocHub, with PDFescape differentiating itself primarily through its long-standing genuinely free online editor rather than a time-limited trial. Because it runs in the browser, PDFescape works across Windows, Mac, and Linux without installation for its free tier, making it a common choice for users who need quick, occasional edits rather than a full-featured, ongoing PDF workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PDFescape is a browser-based PDF editor established in Montreal, Canada, by developers Roger Politis and Bruno Vanryb under the company CTdeveloping, LLC. It has offered a genuinely free online tier for basic editing and form filling since its early years, later being acquired in 2021 by Avanquest, the software division of the publicly traded French group Claranova.

Unlike many competitors that limit their free tier to a short trial, PDFescape's free online editor remains permanently free with usage limits, while Premium and Ultimate subscriptions unlock higher limits and a full desktop application.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The free online editor lets users edit PDF text and images, fill and design PDF forms, and annotate documents entirely within a web browser without installing any software.

The paid desktop app, available with Premium and Ultimate plans, adds advanced tools including PDF creation from over 300 file types, merging, compression, encryption, redaction, and e-signatures, along with unlimited file history on Ultimate.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: PDFescape's free tier allows files up to 10 MB and 100 pages, stores up to 10 files, and retains 7 days of file history, with no watermark added to prints or downloads.

Premium is priced at around $3 per month and raises limits to 40 MB files, 100 stored files, and 30 days of history, while Ultimate at around $6 per month adds unlimited history and the full desktop application with advanced editing tools.

No installation required for the free browser-based editor
Affordable Premium and Ultimate subscription pricing

Backed by publicly traded Claranova, adding financial stability
cons: Free tier limits file size to 10MB, 100 pages, and 10 stored files
Free tier only retains file history for 7 days
Interface feels dated compared to newer competitors
Advanced tools like redaction and encryption require a paid plan

faqs.question: Is PDFescape really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the online editor has a genuinely free tier for basic editing and form filling, though with file size and storage limits.

faqs.question: Who owns PDFescape?
faqs.answer: PDFescape is owned by Avanquest, the software division of the publicly traded French group Claranova, which acquired it in March 2021.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PDFescape Premium and Ultimate?
faqs.answer: Premium removes ads and raises free-tier limits; Ultimate adds unlimited file history and a full desktop app with advanced tools.

faqs.question: Do I need to install PDFescape?
faqs.answer: No, the free tier runs entirely in your web browser; a desktop app is available with Premium and Ultimate.

faqs.question: What are PDFescape's free tier limits?
faqs.answer: Files up to 10MB and 100 pages, a maximum of 10 stored files, and 7 days of file history.

faqs.question: Does PDFescape support e-signatures?
faqs.answer: Yes, e-signature tools are available, primarily in the paid desktop app.

faqs.question: How much does PDFescape Premium cost?
faqs.answer: Premium starts at around $3 per month; Ultimate is around $6 per month.

faqs.question: Is PDFescape safe to use?
faqs.answer: PDFescape has operated for many years and is now backed by the publicly traded Claranova group, though users should review its privacy policy before uploading sensitive documents.

enrichment.short_description: Peergos is an open-source, peer-to-peer encrypted file storage, messaging, and social networking platform that started as a side project in 2013 and builds on IPFS.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted or free public beta account)
enrichment.seo_title: Peergos Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Peergos review covering this open-source, peer-to-peer encrypted storage and social network protocol: features, pricing, pros, cons, alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Peergos is an open-source, peer-to-peer encrypted global filesystem and application protocol that began in 2013 as a side project among a group of friends, including co-founder Ian Preston, who met to code together and grew the idea into a broader effort to build a more private alternative to centralized cloud services. Peergos is fully free and open source, licensed under GPL for its clients and AGPL for its server, and it has attracted over 2,400 stars on GitHub. Technically, Peergos uses IPFS, the InterPlanetary File System, for underlying data storage, routing, and retrieval, while encrypting all data client-side with TweetNaCl before it ever leaves the user's device, splitting files into independently encrypted 5MiB chunks. This design means the operator of a Peergos server cannot read the content of stored files, and the project also goes further than most encrypted storage tools by protecting metadata such as filenames, file sizes, directory structure, modification times, and social-graph information from exposure to the server. Beyond file storage, Peergos includes a secure messenger, an encrypted email client and bridge, and a private social network layer that lets users follow or friend one another without revealing their friendship graph to observers. The project has been security-audited by Cure53 and has received support through initiatives like the EU-backed NGI (Next Generation Internet) program. Users can try Peergos through a free public beta hosted at peergos.net, or self-host their own instance using local disk storage or S3-compatible object storage for full control over their data. Peergos targets privacy-conscious individuals, activists, journalists, and organizations that want an alternative to mainstream cloud storage and social platforms operated by large technology companies, positioning it against tools like Dropbox, ownCloud, Nextcloud, and Tresorit for storage, and against centralized social networks for its private social layer. Because it is a volunteer-driven open-source project rather than a commercial company, Peergos has no subscription pricing; its cost is limited to whatever hosting infrastructure a self-hoster chooses to run, or is entirely free when using the public beta.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Peergos began in 2013 as a side project among a group of friends, including co-founder Ian Preston, and has grown into an open-source, peer-to-peer encrypted global filesystem and application protocol. It is fully free and open source, with clients licensed under GPL and the server under AGPL.

Built on top of IPFS for decentralized data storage and routing, Peergos encrypts all data client-side before it ever leaves a user's device, and has been security audited by Cure53, positioning it as a privacy-first alternative to mainstream cloud storage and social platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Peergos encrypts files client-side in independently encrypted 5MiB chunks using TweetNaCl, and goes further than typical encrypted storage tools by hiding metadata such as filenames, file sizes, directory structure, and social-graph information from the server.

Beyond file storage, Peergos includes a secure messenger, an encrypted email client and bridge, and a private social networking layer that lets users follow or friend each other without exposing their friendship graph.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Peergos has no subscription pricing since it is a volunteer-driven open-source project rather than a commercial company. Users can try it through a free public beta hosted at peergos.net.
Alternatively, users and organizations can self-host their own Peergos instance using local disk storage or S3-compatible object storage, in which case the only cost is their own infrastructure.

features.title: Client-side encryption
features.description: All files are encrypted on the user's device before ever reaching the server, using TweetNaCl in 5MiB chunks.

features.title: Metadata protection
features.description: Filenames, file sizes, directory structure, and social graph are hidden from the server, not just file contents.

features.title: IPFS-based storage
features.description: Uses the InterPlanetary File System for decentralized data storage, routing, and retrieval.

features.title: Secure messenger
features.description: Built-in encrypted messaging between Peergos users.

features.title: Encrypted email client and bridge
features.description: Send and receive encrypted email through Peergos.

features.title: Private social networking
features.description: Follow or friend other users without exposing the friendship graph publicly.

features.title: Fine-grained access control
features.description: Securely share specific files or folders with chosen contacts.

features.title: Self-hosting support
features.description: Run your own Peergos server with local disk or S3-compatible storage.

pros: Strong, audited (Cure53) end-to-end encryption of both data and metadata
Fully open source under GPL/AGPL licenses
Combines file storage, messaging, email, and social networking in one privacy-first platform
Self-hosting option gives full control over infrastructure
Free public beta available for users who do not want to self-host
cons: Smaller community and ecosystem compared to mainstream cloud storage providers
Self-hosting requires technical setup and ongoing maintenance
Feature set and polish are less mature than commercial alternatives like Dropbox or Tresorit
Limited mobile app maturity compared to established competitors

faqs.question: Is Peergos free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Peergos is free and open source; the public beta is free to use, and self-hosting only costs your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: What encryption does Peergos use?
faqs.answer: Peergos encrypts data client-side with TweetNaCl before upload, and also protects metadata like filenames and directory structure.

faqs.question: What is Peergos built on?
faqs.answer: Peergos uses IPFS for decentralized storage, routing, and retrieval of encrypted data.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Peergos?
faqs.answer: Yes, Peergos can be self-hosted using local disk storage or S3-compatible object storage.

faqs.question: Does Peergos have social networking features?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a private social layer for following or friending other users without exposing the friend graph.

faqs.question: Has Peergos been security audited?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project has been audited by the security firm Cure53.

faqs.question: When did Peergos start?
faqs.answer: Peergos began as a side project in 2013.

faqs.question: What license does Peergos use?
faqs.answer: Peergos clients are licensed under GPL and the server under AGPL.

enrichment.short_description: PeerTube is a free, open-source, decentralized video hosting platform using ActivityPub federation and peer-to-peer streaming, developed since 2017 with support from the French non-profit Framasoft.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted); some hosted instances offer paid storage options
enrichment.seo_title: PeerTube Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PeerTube review covering this open-source, federated video hosting platform: features, self-hosting, pricing, pros, cons, and YouTube alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PeerTube is a free and open-source video hosting platform designed as a decentralized alternative to YouTube, Vimeo, and Dailymotion. It was started in 2017 by a French developer known by the handle Chocobozzz as a personal project, and development has since been supported by Framasoft, a French non-profit association headquartered in Lyon that promotes free software and digital commons education. Framasoft hired the original developer and later ran successful crowdfunding campaigns, including one that raised over 53,000 euros in 2018, more than double its goal, and another that reached a 60,000 euro target by October 2020, partly thanks to a 10,000 euro donation from the Debian project. PeerTube's first beta shipped in March 2018 and its first stable 1.0 release arrived in October 2018; by mid-2018 it already had over 100 independent instances collectively hosting more than 10,000 videos. Rather than relying on one centralized company running all the infrastructure, PeerTube lets anyone run their own instance, and instances can federate with one another using the W3C ActivityPub protocol, the same standard used by Mastodon, so that videos, comments, and subscriptions can be discovered and interacted with across independently operated servers as part of the wider Fediverse. To reduce bandwidth costs for instance operators, PeerTube also uses peer-to-peer streaming via WebRTC and HLS, letting viewers share video data with each other while watching. The software is licensed under the AGPLv3+ and is available in around 28 languages through community translation. PeerTube's target users are content creators, communities, universities, media organizations, and activist groups who want to avoid centralized platform moderation, algorithmic recommendation systems, and advertising, or who want to control their own video infrastructure and monetization. There is no central PeerTube company selling subscriptions; instead, the software itself is free, and pricing only applies if a user chooses to pay for storage or hosting on a third-party PeerTube instance rather than running their own server for free. Its main points of comparison are YouTube and Vimeo for centralized video hosting, and Owncast or PixelFed for other Fediverse-native media platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PeerTube is a free and open-source video hosting platform started in 2017 by developer Chocobozzz and supported since by Framasoft, a French non-profit association headquartered in Lyon that promotes free software and digital commons education. The first stable 1.0 release arrived in October 2018.

Rather than one company running centralized infrastructure, PeerTube lets anyone host their own instance, with independent instances federating together through the ActivityPub protocol as part of the wider Fediverse alongside platforms like Mastodon.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: PeerTube supports both uploaded video-on-demand content and live streaming, organized into channels and playlists similar to mainstream video platforms. Instance administrators control their own moderation policies, federation choices, and comment settings.
To reduce hosting costs, PeerTube uses peer-to-peer streaming via WebRTC and HLS, letting viewers share video data with one another while watching, easing the bandwidth burden on any single server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: PeerTube itself is free, open-source software licensed under AGPLv3+, with no central company charging a subscription for the platform. Anyone can download and self-host it at no software cost.

Costs only arise from the infrastructure needed to run an instance, or from choosing to use a third-party PeerTube instance that charges for storage, bandwidth, or hosting rather than running your own server.

features.title: Federated instances (ActivityPub)
features.description: Independent PeerTube servers can interconnect, letting users discover and interact with videos across the Fediverse.

features.title: Peer-to-peer streaming
features.description: WebRTC and HLS-based P2P playback reduces bandwidth costs for instance operators.

features.title: Self-hosting
features.description: Anyone can run their own PeerTube instance and set their own moderation and content rules.

features.title: Live streaming
features.description: Supports live video broadcasting in addition to uploaded video-on-demand content.

features.title: Playlists and channels
features.description: Organize videos into channels and playlists similar to mainstream video platforms.

features.title: Content moderation tools
features.description: Instance admins control federation, comments, and content policies for their server.

features.title: No centralized advertising or recommendation algorithm
features.description: Avoids the ad-driven recommendation model used by mainstream video platforms.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: Available in roughly 28 languages through community translations.

pros: Fully free and open source with no centralized company controlling the network
Decentralized and federated design avoids single points of control or failure
Peer-to-peer streaming reduces hosting bandwidth costs
Instance operators set their own moderation and community rules
Backed by Framasoft, an established non-profit with a multi-year development track record
cons: Requires self-hosting or finding a third-party instance, unlike a single unified platform
Smaller audience reach than YouTube, which can limit content discovery
Setup and server maintenance require technical knowledge
Feature parity with YouTube on recommendation and monetization tools is limited

faqs.question: Is PeerTube free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the software is free and open source; costs only arise if you choose to pay for storage or hosting on someone else's instance.

faqs.question: Who created PeerTube?
faqs.answer: A developer known as Chocobozzz started PeerTube in 2017, with development later supported by the French non-profit Framasoft.

faqs.question: How is PeerTube different from YouTube?
faqs.answer: PeerTube is decentralized: instead of one company hosting all videos, independent instances federate together using ActivityPub, with no central ads or recommendation algorithm.

faqs.question: Can I run my own PeerTube server?
faqs.answer: Yes, PeerTube is designed to be self-hosted by anyone with server infrastructure.

faqs.question: Does PeerTube support live streaming?
faqs.answer: Yes, PeerTube supports live video broadcasts in addition to on-demand uploads.

faqs.question: What license does PeerTube use?
faqs.answer: PeerTube is licensed under the AGPLv3+.

faqs.question: What is Framasoft's role in PeerTube?
faqs.answer: Framasoft, a Lyon-based French non-profit, hired PeerTube's original developer and funds ongoing development through crowdfunding and grants.

faqs.question: Does PeerTube use peer-to-peer technology?
faqs.answer: Yes, it uses WebRTC and HLS-based peer-to-peer streaming to reduce bandwidth load on instance servers.

use_cases.description: Creators who want to avoid platform demonetization or algorithmic suppression host their own PeerTube channel or join a community instance.

use_cases.title: Institutional and educational video hosting

use_cases.title: Community and activist media platforms
use_cases.description: Community groups and activist organizations use PeerTube instances to publish video content outside of corporate platform moderation.
